epigraph.text:
There is a time for everything, and a season for every 
activity under the heavens.
epigraph.quote: Nature is written in mathematical language.

content.text: {Nature is written in mathematical language.}{Galileo Galilei}

When we solve an equation like $x + 3 = 7$, we find exactly one value: 
$x = 4$. But many real-world situations don't have a single answer—they 
have a *range* of acceptable answers. A speed limit sign doesn't say 
you must drive exactly 65 miles per hour; it says you must drive 
*at most* 65 miles per hour. A recipe might say to bake a cake 
*at least* 25 minutes. A doctor might prescribe a dosage 
*between* 200 and 400 milligrams.

These situations call for a different mathematical tool: the 
**inequality**.
Inequality Symbols and Their Meanings

An inequality is a mathematical statement that compares two quantities 
using one of four symbols. Unlike the equals sign, which indicates that 
two quantities have the same value, inequality symbols indicate that one 
quantity is larger or smaller than another.

content.title: Inequality Symbols
content.content:
The four basic inequality symbols are:

| Symbol | Reading | Meaning |
| $<$ | "is less than" | The left side is smaller than the right side |
| $>$ | "is greater than" | The left side is larger than the right side |
| $\leq$ | "is less than or equal to" | The left side is smaller than or equal to the right |
| $\geq$ | "is greater than or equal to" | The left side is larger than or equal to the right |

content.title: Reading inequality symbols correctly
content.content:
The inequality symbol always "points to" the smaller quantity. Think of 
the symbol as an arrow pointing toward the smaller value, or as a hungry 
alligator that always wants to eat the larger number.

- $3 < 7$ means "3 is less than 7" (the arrow points to 3, the smaller value)

- $10 > 4$ means "10 is greater than 4" (the arrow points to 4, the smaller value)

content.text:
The symbols $\leq$ and $\geq$ include the possibility of equality. For 
example, $x \leq 5$ means "$x$ is less than 5 *or* $x$ equals 5." 
This distinction becomes important when we graph solutions and when we 
apply inequalities to real-world situations.

content.title: Interpreting inequality statements
content.problem:
Write each statement as an inequality and determine whether it is true or false.

1. Negative two is less than negative five.

2. Seven is greater than or equal to seven.

3. Three is less than or equal to eight.
content.solution:
1. $-2 < -5$. This is **false**. On the number line, $-2$ is to 
 the *right* of $-5$, so $-2$ is actually *greater than* $-5$.

2. $7 \geq 7$. This is **true**. The symbol $\geq$ means 
 "greater than *or equal to*," and since $7 = 7$, the statement 
 is satisfied.

3. $3 \leq 8$. This is **true**. Since $3 < 8$, the "less than" 
 part is satisfied, making the entire statement true.

content.text: Solutions of Inequalities

When we solve an equation, we find the specific value(s) that make the 
equation true. When we solve an inequality, we find all the values that 
make the inequality true—typically an infinite collection of numbers.

content.title: Solution of an Inequality
content.content:
A **solution** of an inequality is any value that, when substituted 
for the variable, makes the inequality a true statement. The 
**solution set** is the collection of all solutions.

content.title: Checking solutions of an inequality
content.problem:
Determine whether each value is a solution of the inequality $x + 3 > 7$.

1. $x = 5$

2. $x = 4$

3. $x = 10$
content.solution:
We substitute each value and check whether the resulting statement is true.

1. $5 + 3 > 7 \implies 8 > 7$. **True**, so $x = 5$ is a solution. 

2. $4 + 3 > 7 \implies 7 > 7$. **False** (7 is not greater than 7), 
 so $x = 4$ is *not* a solution.

3. $10 + 3 > 7 \implies 13 > 7$. **True**, so $x = 10$ is a solution.

content.text:
Notice that the inequality $x + 3 > 7$ has infinitely many solutions: 
$x = 5, 6, 7, 8, \ldots$ and also $x = 4.1, 4.01, 4.001, \ldots$ and 
even $x = 100$ or $x = 1{,}000{,}000$. Any value greater than 4 will work. 
We need a way to describe this infinite collection of solutions.
Graphing Inequalities on a Number Line

The most intuitive way to represent the solution set of an inequality is 
with a **number line graph**. We shade the portion of the number line 
containing all solutions and use a special notation at the boundary point.

content.title: Graphing Inequalities
content.content:
To graph an inequality on a number line:

1. Identify the **boundary point** (the number in the inequality).

2. Draw an **open circle** $\circ$ if the boundary is *not* 
 included ($<$ or $>$), or a **closed circle** $\bullet$ if it 
 *is* included ($\leq$ or $\geq$).

3. Shade the portion of the number line containing all solutions:
 itemize

4. Shade to the *left* for $<$ or $\leq$ (smaller values)

5. Shade to the *right* for $>$ or $\geq$ (larger values)
 itemize

content.title: Graphing simple inequalities
content.problem:
Graph each inequality on a number line.

1. $x > -2$

2. $x \leq 4$

3. $x \geq 0$

content.text: Set-Builder and Interval Notation

In addition to graphs, mathematicians use two compact written notations to 
describe solution sets: **set-builder notation** and 
**interval notation**.

content.title: Set-Builder Notation
content.content:
**Set-builder notation** describes a set 
by stating the property its elements must satisfy:
\[
 \{x \mid x > 3\}
\]
This is read "the set of all $x$ such that $x$ is greater than 3." The 
vertical bar $\mid$ means "such that."

content.title: Interval Notation
content.content:
**Interval notation** uses parentheses and 
brackets to describe a set of numbers between two endpoints:

| Interval | Meaning | Inequality |
| $(a, b)$ | All $x$ with $a < x < b$ | Open at both ends |
| $[a, b]$ | All $x$ with $a \leq x \leq b$ | Closed at both ends |
| $(a, b]$ | All $x$ with $a < x \leq b$ | Open at $a$, closed at $b$ |
| $[a, b)$ | All $x$ with $a \leq x < b$ | Closed at $a$, open at $b$ |
| $(a, \infty)$ | All $x$ with $x > a$ | Extends infinitely right |
| $[a, \infty)$ | All $x$ with $x \geq a$ | Extends infinitely right |
| $(-\infty, b)$ | All $x$ with $x < b$ | Extends infinitely left |
| $(-\infty, b]$ | All $x$ with $x \leq b$ | Extends infinitely left |

*Note:** Parentheses indicate the endpoint is *not* 
included; brackets indicate the endpoint *is* included. Infinity 
($\infty$) always gets a parenthesis because infinity is not a number we 
can reach.

content.title: Converting between notations
content.problem:
Write each inequality in set-builder notation and interval notation, then 
graph it.

1. $x \geq -1$

2. $x < 4$

content.text: Translating Verbal Statements to Inequalities

Many real-world problems are described in words. Learning to translate 
these verbal descriptions into mathematical inequalities is a crucial skill.

content.title: Key Phrases for Inequalities
content.content:
| Phrase | Symbol |
| is less than, is fewer than, is under, is below | $<$ |
| is greater than, is more than, exceeds, is above | $>$ |
| is at most, is no more than, does not exceed, maximum | $\leq$ |
| is at least, is no less than, minimum | $\geq$ |

content.title: Translating phrases to inequalities
content.problem:
Translate each statement into an inequality. Let $x$ represent the unknown 
quantity.

1. The speed limit is 65 miles per hour.

2. You must be at least 16 years old to drive.

3. The elevator has a maximum capacity of 2000 pounds.

4. The temperature will stay below freezing.

5. The minimum wage is \$12.50 per hour.
content.solution:
1. If $x$ is your speed, then $x \leq 65$. (You can drive at 65 or any 
 speed less than 65, but not more.)

2. If $x$ is your age, then $x \geq 16$. (You can be 16 or older.)

3. If $x$ is the weight in the elevator, then $x \leq 2000$.

4. If $x$ is the temperature in degrees Fahrenheit, then $x < 32$. 
 (Below freezing means strictly less than 32°F.)

5. If $x$ is the hourly wage, then $x \geq 12.50$.

content.text: Writing Inequalities from Graphs

We can also work in reverse: given a number line graph, write the 
corresponding inequality.

content.title: Writing inequalities from graphs
content.solution:
1. The open circle at 2 means the boundary is *not* included, and 
 shading to the right indicates values greater than 2.
 itemize

2. Inequality: $x > 2$

3. Set-builder: $\{x \mid x > 2\}$

4. Interval: $(2, \infty)$
 itemize

5. The closed circle at $-1$ means the boundary *is* included, 
 and shading to the left indicates values less than or equal to $-1$.
 itemize

6. Inequality: $x \leq -1$

7. Set-builder: $\{x \mid x \leq -1\}$

8. Interval: $(-\infty, -1]$
 itemize

content.title: Thomas Harriot (1560--1621)
content.content:
The inequality symbols $<$ and $>$ were introduced by the English 
mathematician Thomas Harriot in his work *Artis Analyticae Praxis*, 
published posthumously in 1631. Before Harriot, mathematicians wrote out 
phrases like "is less than" in full. Harriot was also a navigator, 
scientist, and explorer who accompanied Sir Walter Raleigh on expeditions 
to the Americas—a reminder that mathematics often advances through people 
who explore many fields.

content.text: Try It Answers
**Try It .1:**
**Try It .2:**

marginNotes.text:
Do not move an ancient boundary stone 
set up by your ancestors.

The boundary lines have fallen for me in 
pleasant places; surely I have a delightful inheritance.

Do not move your neighbor's boundary 
stone set up by your predecessors in the inheritance you receive.

marginNotes.author: Archimedes
marginNotes.text:
Give me a place to stand, and I shall move 
the earth.
